1.exposition: The second half of Revelation opens by pulling back the curtain on the whole of sacred history at once. A great sign appears in heaven — not an omen written in the stars but a heavenly disclosure — a radiant woman clothed with the sun, the moon beneath her feet, crowned with twelve stars, crying out in the pains of birth. Over against her rises another sign: a great red dragon, seven-headed and ten-horned, whose tail sweeps a third of the stars from heaven and who crouches before the woman to devour her child the moment it is born. She bears a son who is to rule all the nations with a rod of iron — and in a single breath the vision carries him from the womb to the throne: the child is caught up to God. Cheated of his prey, the dragon is left only with the mother, who flees to a wilderness God has prepared, to be nourished there through the appointed 1,260 days. Birth, deliverance, flight — the gospel and the church's whole pilgrimage, compressed into six verses.
1.original_language: The scene is a sēmeion ("sign"), not a teras ("wonder"): a symbol charged with meaning, not a marvel to be gawked at. The woman is peribeblēmenē ton hēlion — "clothed with the sun," the perfect participle hinting at a glory permanently put on. Her child is arsen ("male"), destined to poimainein ("to shepherd, rule") the nations "with a rod of iron," the words lifted straight from the Greek of Psalm 2:9. And he is hērpasthē (from harpazō, "to snatch, catch up") to God's throne in v.5 — the verb marking the child's rescue as a divine act, not a mere escape.
1.historical_context: Ancient readers would have recognized the shape of the story. The Greco-Roman world told of a great dragon (Python) who pursued the pregnant goddess Leto to devour her child, only for the child (Apollo) to escape and later destroy the dragon — a "combat myth" the Roman emperors borrowed to cast themselves as the divine child who slays chaos. John takes the familiar plot and overturns it: the true child who defeats the dragon is not Caesar but the crucified and risen Christ. Beneath even that lies the oldest promise of all — Genesis 3:15, the enmity between the serpent and the seed of the woman — now reaching its appointed crisis. The dragon's seven heads and ten horns gather Daniel's four beasts (Daniel 7:7) into one figure, naming Satan the power standing behind every persecuting empire; and the 1,260 days is Daniel's bounded season of the saints' distress.
1.christ: The male child is Christ, and the vision tells his whole story in one line: born of the covenant people, marked out by the royal title of Psalm 2:9 as the one who will rule the nations, and caught up to God and to his throne — birth, escape, and enthronement with nothing between, because what the vision cares about is that the dragon's lunge missed. The child now reigns; and it is precisely because he is already enthroned that the woman's wilderness protection is secure. The rod of iron he holds is Psalm 2's messianic scepter, the pledge that the nations — and the dragon behind them — belong finally to him.

verses.note: The vision opens with what John calls a great sign in heaven — and, as grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson stress, the word is sēmeion (a sign that means something), not a mere teras (a portent). A woman appears, clothed with the sun, the moon under her feet, crowned with twelve stars. Her identity is the great crux of the chapter, and the witnesses divide. The latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede, with the reformed Gill and grammatical-historical Barnes, read her as the covenant people of God — Victorinus's "ancient Church of fathers and prophets and saints and apostles," clothed with the light of Christ and treading the changeable world (the moon) underfoot, her crown the twelve patriarchs or apostles. The medieval and Roman Catholic tradition has read her rather as the Virgin Mary, who literally bore Christ. wesleyan-arminian Wesley hears behind the sun, moon, and stars the imagery of Joseph's dream (Genesis 37:9) — Israel, from whom the Messiah springs.

She was pregnant and crying out in birth pains. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the technical language of pregnancy and the vivid participles ōdinousa ("in travail") and basanizomenē ("tormented"). grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the anguish as the law of the church's own life: since Genesis 3:16 all life dawns in pain, and the covenant people cannot bring Christ forth into the world — or be formed in his likeness (Galatians 4:19) — without travail. wesleyan-arminian Wesley hears in the cries the sighs and prayers of the saints longing for the Messiah's appearing — the whole Old Testament groaning toward Bethlehem.

Another sign: a great red dragon, with seven heads and ten horns and seven diadems. latin-fathers Victorinus explains the color — red, or purple, for the murderer who "from the beginning" has oppressed the human race. puritan Henry reads a dragon "for strength and terror," red "for fierceness and cruelty," and — with grammatical-historical Barnes — takes the seven heads as the seven hills of Rome, the persecuting empire through which Satan works; the very heads reappear on the beast of chapter 13 and are named seven mountains in 17:9. The ten horns echo Daniel's fourth beast (Daniel 7:7). He is not yet named, but v.9 will unmask him.

The dragon's tail swept a third of the stars from heaven and cast them down — read by grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson of Satan's vast destructive power, whether his dragging down of the fallen angels or his casting down of the church's teachers. Then he takes his post: standing before the woman to devour her child the moment it is born. puritan Henry sees the ancient enmity of Genesis 3:15 coming to its crisis — the serpent, knowing the seed of the woman would crush his head, moves to devour him first; Herod's slaughter of the infants (Matthew 2:16) is that malice made visible.

She bore a male child, one who is to rule all the nations with a rod of iron. The title is a direct quotation of Psalm 2:9 — where the Greek poimanei ("will shepherd") turns the iron rod into the unbreakable rule of the Davidic king — so that, for grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, the child is unmistakably the Messiah. (puritan Henry records a minority reading that took the man-child as a race of true believers destined to reign under Christ, but the Psalm-2 title and the throne settle the point on Christ himself.) Then the verse leaps from cradle to crown: the child was caught up to God and to his throne — harpazō, the verb of the ascension — and, as eastern Cyril reads it, Christ is exalted beyond the dragon's reach, the whole earthly ministry compressed because it is the dragon's defeat, not the life-story, that the vision means to tell.

The woman fled into the wilderness, to a place prepared by God, to be nourished 1,260 days. reformed Gill points to the flight of the Jerusalem Christians to Pella, and beyond it to the church's refuge among the nations; grammatical-historical Barnes reads the 1,260 days as the long era of the church's obscurity and persecution (historicists count 1,260 years). Either way the wilderness is not defeat but provision — the same span as the forty-two months of 11:2 and the "time, times, and half a time" of v.14 — God feeding his people in the desert as he once fed Israel.

witnesses.point: The woman is the ancient Church of fathers, prophets, saints, and apostles, travailing in longing until Christ, the fruit of her people according to the flesh, took flesh from among them.
The woman is the church clothed in the brightness of the gospel, the older dispensation (the moon) beneath her feet; the man-child caught up to heaven pledges the church's ultimate safety.
She is the people of God of both covenants from whom the Messiah came — "certainly not the Virgin Mary, as verse 17 makes clear" — crowned with the twelve tribes or apostles.
Here is the ancient enmity of Genesis 3:15: the red dragon, pagan Rome on its seven hills, stands ready to devour the child the moment it is born.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.point: The vision reworks the widely known combat myth of the goddess and the dragon that threatens her newborn — a story Roman propaganda applied to the emperor — reclaiming it so the true deliverer is the Messiah, not the imperial "son of the gods."

1.application: The church has always been a wilderness people — pursued, yet fed; hidden, yet kept. When your own faith feels driven to the margins, remember that obscurity in Scripture is often the shape of God's protection, not the sign of his absence.

7.exposition: Now the vision reaches behind the persecution to its cause. War breaks out in heaven: Michael and his angels against the dragon and his angels. The dragon is beaten, and there is no longer any place for them above; the great dragon is thrown down — and here at last he is named, that ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan, the deceiver of the whole world. A loud voice answers from heaven, and its song is the theological heart of the book: now the salvation and the power and the kingdom of our God and the authority of his Christ have come, for the accuser of our brothers has been thrown down. How was he thrown down? Not by superior force but by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony, for they loved not their lives even unto death. Heaven is bidden to rejoice; earth and sea are warned, for the defeated dragon comes down in great wrath, because he knows that his time is short.
7.original_language: Verse 9 empties the dragon's whole dossier of names: ho ophis ho archaios ("the ancient serpent"), ho diabolos ("the slanderer"), ho Satanas (the Aramaic "adversary"), and ho planōn ("the deceiver"). In v.10 he is the katēgōr — a rare, rabbinic-sounding form of katēgoros ("prosecuting accuser"), the courtroom counsel who brings the charge. The saints' response is enikēsan ("they conquered"), the aorist of nikaō, the "overcomer" verb that crowns every one of the seven letters (2:7). And the name of their champion, Michaēl, is itself the retort to the dragon's pride: "Who is like God?"
7.historical_context: Michael is Israel's angelic champion in Daniel 10:13, 21 and the great prince who stands for God's people in Daniel 12:1; the Qumran War Scroll pictures a final battle between the sons of light under Michael and the forces of darkness — the same heavenly-warfare tradition John draws on. "The accuser" belongs to the older courtroom scenes of Job 1:6-12 and Zechariah 3:1-2, where the adversary stands in the divine council to prosecute the righteous. And the casting-down echoes Isaiah 14:12 and, most pointedly, Jesus' own words: "I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven" (Luke 10:18).
7.christ: Heaven's song grounds the whole victory in the cross. The dragon is not so much out-fought as out-bled: they conquered him by the blood of the Lamb. The accuser's entire office is to point at sin as grounds for condemnation; the blood of the Lamb removes the grounds, and with them his standing. So the "now" of v.10 — "now the salvation of our God has come" — is the eschatological now opened by the cross and resurrection, when the one who was slain was caught up to the throne (v.5) and the courtroom of heaven was cleared of its prosecutor.

verses.note: War arose in heaven: Michael and his angels fighting against the dragon. Who is Michael? grammatical-historical Ellicott, with Barnes, identifies him as the archangel who champions God's people in Daniel 10:13 and stands guard over them in the final distress of Daniel 12:1. puritan Henry leans the other way, taking Michael ("who is like God?") as a title of Christ himself, "the great Angel of the covenant," captain of the host that fights for the church. The two armies — angelic and demonic — are drawn up, and the church's strength, Henry adds, lies not in numbers but in her captain.

He was defeated, and there was no longer any place for them in heaven. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the completeness of it: the dragon, for all his power, could not prevail, and lost his very standing. The accuser who had enjoyed access to the heavenly court — as in Job 1:6 and Zechariah 3:1 — is evicted from it.

Now the mask comes off: the great dragon was thrown down, that ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan, the deceiver of the whole world. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson unfold the piled-up names — ho ophis ho archaios, the serpent of Eden (Genesis 3); diabolos, the slanderer; Satanas, the adversary; ho planōn, the one who leads the whole inhabited world astray. puritan Henry notes that he is cast down "and his angels with him" — a decisive overthrow, though his rage on earth (vv.12-13) is not yet spent. Jesus had already seen it: "I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven" (Luke 10:18).

A loud voice: Now the salvation and the power and the kingdom of our God and the authority of his Christ have come. The cause of the joy is the silencing of the prosecutor: the accuser of our brothers is thrown down, who accused them day and night before our God. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the courtroom term katēgōr — the rare, rabbinic-flavored form of katēgoros, the prosecuting counsel. puritan Henry pictures Satan as the tireless indictment-bringer of Job 1 and Zechariah 3, willing even to endure God's presence for the sake of accusing the saints — over against whom Christ stands as their Advocate.

The verse the whole book turns on: they conquered him by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony, and they loved not their lives even unto death. puritan Henry names the three: the meritorious cause is the blood of the Lamb — "Christ by dying destroyed him that had the power of death"; the instrument is the word of their testimony, the sword of the Spirit wielded in fearless witness; and the seal is their willingness to die, loving not their lives when love of life would have cost them Christ. eastern Cyril hears the martyr's spirit. The verb is enikēsan — "they conquered," the overcomer's word promised to each of the seven churches (2:7). The blood that pardons is also the blood that defeats the accuser, for it removes the very ground of the accusation.

Rejoice, O heavens — but woe to the earth and the sea. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson set the two responses side by side: heaven is glad at the accuser's fall, but his expulsion turns his fury downward. puritan Henry catches the terrible logic of the last clause — the devil has come down in great wrath, because he knows that his time is short: his rage is real and dangerous, yet it is the rage of the already-defeated, fiercer precisely because he knows the clock is running out.

witnesses.point: The victory is won by the blood of the Lamb as the meritorious cause and the word of testimony as the instrument, sealed by saints who loved not their lives unto death; Michael he reads as Christ, captain of the church's salvation.
The accuser is the katēgōr, the prosecuting counsel of the heavenly court, now cast down so that he can charge the brethren no longer.
The overcomers did not cling to their lives but were willing to die for Christ; the martyr's self-sacrifice seals their triumph.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: The victory-formula of v.11 recasts holy war: the saints share the Lamb's conquest not by taking life but by faithful witness and, if need be, by laying down their own lives — Christ's death, not military might, is the weapon that casts the accuser down.

7.application: The accuser still whispers his indictments, but he no longer has a courtroom. When conscience or the enemy arraigns you, the answer is not a better defense but a better Advocate — the blood of the Lamb, which has already emptied the charge. That, and faithful confession held even at cost, is how the battle is actually won.

13.exposition: Cast to earth, the dragon turns to the one target left him. Unable to touch the enthroned child, he pursues the woman who bore him. But she is given the two wings of the great eagle and flies to her wilderness place, to be nourished for a time, and times, and half a time, out of the serpent's reach. He spews a river from his mouth to sweep her away, but the earth opens and swallows the flood. Foiled again, the dragon does the only thing left him: he goes off to make war on the rest of her offspring — those who keep the commandments of God and hold to the testimony of Jesus. With that sentence the vision hands the reader over to the chapters that follow: the beast, the mark, the long war on the saints. The woman herself is safe; her scattered children are where the battle now falls — and they are exactly the seven churches to whom John writes.
13.original_language: The wilderness season is kairon kai kairous kai hēmisu kairou — "a time, and times, and half a time," the cadence borrowed verbatim from Daniel 7:25 for the bounded span of the saints' oppression. The woman's other children are her sperma ("seed, offspring") — the serpent's ancient enemy of Genesis 3:15. And what marks them is hē martyria Iēsou, "the testimony of Jesus," a phrase that hovers between the testimony they bear about Jesus and the testimony Jesus himself bore, the faithful witness (1:5) whose witness theirs continues.
13.historical_context: The imagery is a new exodus. The "two wings of a great eagle" recall Exodus 19:4 — "I bore you on eagles' wings" — and the mother-eagle of Deuteronomy 32:11; the wilderness and the Danielic three-and-a-half years (Daniel 7:25) frame the church's pilgrimage as Israel's desert journey retold. The earth opening to "swallow" the flood echoes the ground that swallowed Korah's rebellion (Numbers 16:32). And "those who keep the commandments of God and hold to the testimony of Jesus" (v.17) is simply Revelation's standing description of the seven churches it has addressed from the first chapter.
13.christ: The dragon's war is finally a war against Christ's own witness in the world. To hold the testimony of Jesus is to carry on the confession of the faithful witness himself (1:5); the martyrs under the altar were slain for exactly this (6:9). So the offspring's battle is not theirs alone — it extends the testimony of the enthroned child, and its outcome was settled the moment he was caught up to the throne (v.5). The dragon fights on, but he fights a rear-guard action against a victory already won.

verses.note: When the dragon saw that he had been thrown down, he pursued the woman who had given birth to the male child. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the redirection: foiled against the exalted Christ (v.5), Satan vents his fury on the church. puritan Henry notes the pointedness of it — she is attacked precisely as the mother of the Messiah, hated for the Son she bore.

The woman was given the two wings of the great eagle, to fly into the wilderness. grammatical-historical Robertson hears the Exodus behind it — Exodus 19:4, "I bore you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself" (cf. Deuteronomy 32:11). She is nourished there for a time, and times, and half a time — the very phrase of Daniel 7:25, three and a half "times," the same span as the 1,260 days of v.6. eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical Barnes underline the point: she is kept "from the face of the serpent," borne to safety and fed — not lifted out of danger, but sustained through it.

The serpent poured water like a river out of his mouth after the woman, to sweep her away. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the flood as an overwhelming assault — persecution, or floods of error and heresy, or hostile armies and peoples (in Revelation "waters" are peoples, 17:15) — every torrent Satan can loose at once to drown the church.

But the earth helped the woman, and opened its mouth and swallowed the river. grammatical-historical Barnes sees a providential rescue — as if creation itself takes the church's side, the persecuting flood drained away (historicists point to the breakup of the powers that loosed it). The image recalls the earth opening to swallow Korah's rebellion (Numbers 16:32): the very creation the dragon would weaponize turns against his own instrument.

Thwarted, the dragon does the last thing left him: furious with the woman, he goes off to make war on the rest of her offspring — those who keep the commandments of God and hold to the testimony of Jesus. grammatical-historical Barnes notes that here Revelation tells us plainly who the "seed" are: the true church, marked by obedience and by hē martyria Iēsou, the testimony of Jesus. reformed Gill reads the dragon, stripped of his old imperial power, now turning on the scattered and more vulnerable members. The war that fills the rest of the book begins here — and, as the enthroned child has already guaranteed (v.5), its end is not in doubt.

witnesses.point: Revelation itself names the woman's seed — those who keep God's commandments and hold the testimony of Jesus, the true church; the eagle's wings picture her swift, God-given escape, as Israel found refuge in the wilderness.
The eagle's wings echo Exodus 19:4; the "time, times, and half a time" is Daniel's three-and-a-half years, the same span as the 1,260 days.
Stripped of his former imperial power, the dragon turns to war on the scattered and weaker members of the church, the remnant of the woman's seed.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: The woman's "rest of her offspring" are the messianic community of the church age; her wilderness is the whole period of protected pilgrimage between Christ's ascension and return, and the dragon's turn to war on the saints is what drives the beast's persecution in the chapters that follow.

13.application: If you are one of the woman's offspring, the pressure you feel is not a sign that evil is winning; it is the frustrated fury of a dragon already thrown down — denied both the child and the mother, reduced to skirmishing against her children. Keep the commandments; hold the testimony. The war is real, but its verdict is not pending.

_meta.title: The Woman, the Child, and the Dragon
reflection: The saints "conquered him by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony, and they loved not their lives even unto death" (v.11). What would it look like to fight your real battles with those weapons rather than the world's?

The woman is kept in the wilderness "for a time, and times, and half a time" (v.14) — protected, but not spared. Where are you asking God to rescue you from a trouble when he is offering to sustain you through it?

Behind the pressures the churches faced stood a defeated dragon raging "because he knows that his time is short" (v.12). How does seeing the enemy as already-defeated change the way you face opposition to your faith?
